title: 暗物质观测与探测技术文献验证
objective: 验证 ZhiZhi 对暗物质观测方法、探测技术、实验约束和天体物理信号的文献检索、结构化提取和知识缺口探测能力.

papergraph.title: Astrophysical uncertainties on the local dark matter distribution and direct detection experiments
papergraph.citation: A. Green (2017) Astrophysical uncertainties on the local dark matter distribution and direct detection experiments doi:10.1088/1361-6471/aa7819
papergraph.abstract: The differential event rate in weakly interacting massive particle (WIMP) direct detection experiments depends on the local dark matter density and velocity distribution. Accurate modelling of the local dark matter distribution is therefore required to obtain reliable constraints on the WIMP particle physics properties. Data analyses typically use a simple standard halo model which might not be a good approximation to the real Milky Way (MW) halo. We review observational determinations of the local dark matter density, circular speed and escape speed and also studies of the local dark matter distribution in simulated MW-like galaxies. We discuss the effects of the uncertainties in these quantities on the energy spectrum and its time and direction dependence. Finally, we conclude with an overview of various methods for handling these astrophysical uncertainties.
papergraph.method: review of observational determinations and simulated Milky Way-like galaxies
papergraph.scenario: local dark matter distribution in the Milky Way halo
papergraph.benchmark: energy spectrum, time dependence, direction dependence
papergraph.contribution: systematic assessment of astrophysical uncertainties affecting WIMP direct detection interpretations
credibility_reasons: credibility multiplied down by low publication quality

papergraph.title: The complementary of CTAO, direct detection and collider searches for dark matter in Effective Field Theories and Simplified Models
papergraph.citation: I. Reis et al. (2025) The complementary of CTAO, direct detection and collider searches for dark matter in Effective Field Theories and Simplified Models doi:10.1088/1475-7516/2026/02/015
papergraph.venue: Journal of Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics
papergraph.abstract: This paper explores the sensitivity of the Cherenkov Telescope Array Observatory to dark matter annihilation in the Galactic Center, within the frameworks of Effective Field Theory and Simplified Models. We present sensitivity forecasts, utilizing an up-to-date instrument configuration and incorporating the latest models for Galactic Diffuse Emission. A key aspect of our work is the inclusion of updated dark matter density profiles, J-factors, and velocity dispersion distributions derived from the FIRE-2 cosmological hydrodynamical simulations, which significantly impact the expected indirect detection signals. Furthermore, we update the constraints from direct detection experiments (Xenon1T and LZ) taking into account the astrophysical uncertainties informed by the FIRE-2 simulations, and also investigate limits coming from collider searches (ATLAS and CMS). Our analysis reveals improved constraints on the effective suppression scale (M *) in the Effective Field Theory framework and on the mediator mass (M med) in Simplified Models compared to previous studies, highlighting the complementarity of the Cherenkov Telescope Array Observatory with direct and collider searches in probing a wide range of dark matter scenarios. We discuss the implications of these results for various dark matter interaction types, including scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, and axial-vector mediators, and emphasize the importance of considering realistic astrophysical inputs in interpreting dark matter search results across different experimental fronts.
strengths: incorporation of FIRE-2 cosmological hydrodynamical simulations for dark matter density profiles, J-factors, and velocity dispersion
updated astrophysical uncertainties for direct detection constraints
complementarity analysis across indirect (CTAO), direct (Xenon1T, LZ), and collider (ATLAS, CMS) searches
improvements: improved constraints on effective suppression scale (M*) in Effective Field Theory
improved constraints on mediator mass (M_med) in Simplified Models
papergraph.method: sensitivity forecasting using instrument simulation and astrophysical modeling
papergraph.scenario: dark matter annihilation in the Galactic Center
papergraph.benchmark: constraints on effective suppression scale (M*) and mediator mass (M_med)
papergraph.contribution: improved constraints on M* and M_med via combined CTAO, direct detection, and collider analysis with FIRE-2–informed astrophysical inputs

papergraph.title: The local dark matter phase-space density and impact on WIMP direct detection
papergraph.citation: R. Catena et al. (2011) The local dark matter phase-space density and impact on WIMP direct detection doi:10.1088/1475-7516/2012/05/005
papergraph.abstract: We present a new determination of the local dark matter phase-space density. This result is obtained implementing, in the limit of isotropic velocity distribution and spherical symmetry, Eddington's inversion formula, which links univocally the dark matter distribution function to the density profile, and applying, within a Bayesian framework, a Markov Chain Monte Carlo algorithm to sample mass models for the Milky Way against a broad and variegated sample of dynamical constraints. We consider three possible choices for the dark matter density profile, namely the Einasto, NFW and Burkert profiles, finding that the velocity dispersion, which characterizes the width in the distribution, tends to be larger for the Burkert case, while the escape velocity depends very weakly on the profile, with the mean value we obtain being in very good agreement with estimates from stellar kinematics. The derived dark matter phase-space densities differ significantly — most dramatically in the high velocity tails — from the model usually taken as a reference in dark matter detection studies, a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution with velocity dispersion fixed in terms of the local circular velocity and with a sharp truncation at a given value of the escape velocity. We discuss the impact of astrophysical uncertainties on dark matter scattering rates and direct detection exclusion limits, considering a few sample cases and showing that the most sensitive ones are those for light dark matter particles and for particles scattering inelastically. As a general trend, regardless of the assumed profile, when adopting a self-consistent phase-space density, we find that rates are larger, and hence exclusion limits stronger, than with the standard Maxwell-Boltzmann approximation. Tools for applying our result on the local dark matter phase-space density to other dark matter candidates or experimental setups are provided.
papergraph.method: Eddington's inversion formula + Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling
papergraph.scenario: local dark matter phase-space density in the Milky Way
papergraph.benchmark: dark matter scattering rates, direct detection exclusion limits
papergraph.contribution: New self-consistent determination of local dark matter phase-space density differing significantly from standard Maxwell-Boltzmann approximation, leading to larger scattering rates and stronger exclusion limits
credibility_reasons: credibility multiplied down by low publication quality

papergraph.title: WIMP and FIMP dark matter in the inert doublet plus singlet model
papergraph.citation: G. Bélanger et al. (2022) WIMP and FIMP dark matter in the inert doublet plus singlet model doi:10.1103/PhysRevD.106.095019
papergraph.abstract: We consider multi-component dark matter in a model where one dark matter component is feebly interacting (FIMP) while the second is weakly interacting (WIMP). The model contains an inert scalar doublet and a complex scalar singlet and features a discrete Z 4 symmetry. We determine the parameter space that satisﬁes theoretical constraints, collider constraints, relic density as well as direct and indirect detection limits. We discuss the possibility to probe the model through collider and astrophysical searches. We ﬁnd that it is possible that the FIMP forms the dominant DM component, in this case the astrophysical signatures of the WIMP are much suppressed. We also explore the case where the doublet can decay into pairs of singlets leaving only one DM component, the FIMP. These scenarios are constrained by BBN and are best explored at colliders since astrophysical signatures are suppressed.
papergraph.method: parameter space scan with theoretical and experimental constraints
papergraph.scenario: multi-component dark matter in inert doublet plus complex singlet model with Z4 symmetry
papergraph.contribution: Identification of viable parameter space where FIMP dominates dark matter abundance and WIMP signatures are suppressed, including cases where inert doublet decays to singlets yielding single-component FIMP DM
papergraph.limitation: Astrophysical signatures of the WIMP are much suppressed when FIMP dominates, limiting detectability via indirect and direct detection
credibility_reasons: credibility multiplied down by low publication quality

keynotes.title: Astrophysical uncertainties on the local dark matter distribution and direct detection experiments

keynote.title: Astrophysical uncertainties on the local dark matter distribution and direct detection experiments
keynote.core_problem: Uncertainties in the local dark matter density and velocity distribution affect the reliability of WIMP parameter constraints from direct detection experiments, and the standard halo model may poorly approximate the true Milky Way halo.
contributions: Review of observational determinations of local dark matter density, circular speed, and escape speed
Review of studies of local dark matter distribution in simulated Milky Way-like galaxies
Analysis of how astrophysical uncertainties affect the energy spectrum and its time/direction dependence in direct detection
Overview of methods for handling astrophysical uncertainties in data analyses
methods: Literature review and synthesis of observational and simulation-based constraints
Analysis of impact of astrophysical parameters on differential event rate predictions
assumptions: Differential event rate depends on local dark matter density and velocity distribution
Standard halo model is commonly used but potentially inadequate

important_claims.claim: The differential event rate in WIMP direct detection experiments depends on the local dark matter density and velocity distribution.
important_claims.evidence: Stated as foundational premise in abstract.

important_claims.claim: The standard halo model might not be a good approximation to the real Milky Way halo.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a structured survey of astrophysical uncertainties relevant for interpreting direct detection results; serves as a reference for incorporating or marginalizing over halo model uncertainties in likelihood analyses.

keynotes.title: The complementary of CTAO, direct detection and collider searches for dark matter in Effective Field Theories and Simplified Models

keynote.title: The complementary of CTAO, direct detection and collider searches for dark matter in Effective Field Theories and Simplified Models
keynote.core_problem: How to jointly interpret and improve constraints on dark matter parameter space using complementary search strategies (indirect via CTAO, direct via Xenon1T/LZ, and collider via ATLAS/CMS), while accounting for realistic astrophysical uncertainties.
contributions: Sensitivity forecasts for CTAO to dark matter annihilation in the Galactic Center using updated instrument configuration and Galactic Diffuse Emission models

Incorporation of FIRE-2 cosmological hydrodynamical simulation outputs—specifically updated dark matter density profiles, J-factors, and velocity dispersion distributions—into indirect, direct, and collider analyses
Updated direct detection constraints (Xenon1T, LZ) and collider limits (ATLAS, CMS) that consistently propagate FIRE-2–informed astrophysical uncertainties
Improved joint constraints on effective suppression scale (M*) in EFT and mediator mass (M_med) in Simplified Models compared to prior work
Systematic comparison across interaction types (scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, axial-vector mediators) highlighting complementarity
methods: Indirect detection sensitivity forecasting for CTAO using Monte Carlo simulations and background modeling with updated Galactic Diffuse Emission
J-factor calculation using FIRE-2–derived dark matter density profiles and line-of-sight integration
Propagation of FIRE-2–informed astrophysical uncertainties (e.g., velocity dispersion, halo profile) into direct detection recoil spectra and collider production cross sections
Combined likelihood or exclusion limit analysis across CTAO, direct detection (Xenon1T, LZ), and collider (ATLAS, CMS) datasets
experiments_or_evidence: CTAO sensitivity forecasts for Galactic Center dark matter annihilation
Revised exclusion limits from Xenon1T and LZ experiments incorporating FIRE-2 astrophysical inputs
Revised collider limits from ATLAS and CMS analyses informed by FIRE-2 velocity and density distributions
assumptions: Dark matter annihilates in the Galactic Center according to EFT or Simplified Model frameworks
FIRE-2 simulations provide physically representative astrophysical inputs (density profiles, J-factors, velocity dispersions) for Milky Way–like halos
Standard astrophysical backgrounds (e.g., Galactic diffuse gamma-ray emission) are adequately modeled
Detector response and systematic uncertainties for CTAO, Xenon1T/LZ, and ATLAS/CMS are sufficiently characterized
datasets_or_materials: FIRE-2 cosmological hydrodynamical simulation outputs (density profiles, J-factors, velocity dispersion distributions)
Xenon1T and LZ experimental data and analysis frameworks
ATLAS and CMS collider search results (e.g., mono-X, dilepton + MET)

important_claims.claim: FIRE-2–informed astrophysical inputs significantly impact expected indirect detection signals and derived constraints.
important_claims.evidence: Updated J-factors and velocity dispersion distributions from FIRE-2 are explicitly incorporated and shown to alter sensitivity forecasts and exclusion limits.

important_claims.claim: CTAO provides complementary constraints to direct detection and collider searches across EFT and Simplified Model parameter spaces.
important_claims.evidence: Joint analysis yields improved constraints on M* (EFT) and M_med (Simplified Models) compared to previous studies, with distinct sensitivity regions per interaction type.

important_claims.claim: Realistic astrophysical modeling is essential for consistent interpretation across search channels.
important_claims.evidence: Astrophysical uncertainties from FIRE-2 are propagated uniformly into indirect, direct, and collider analyses, enabling fair cross-experiment comparison.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a template for consistent astrophysical uncertainty propagation across dark matter search frontiers; enables reproducible, multi-messenger constraint mapping using FIRE-2–based halo properties; supports future joint analyses with upcoming CTAO data releases and next-generation direct/collider experiments.

keynotes.title: The local dark matter phase-space density and impact on WIMP direct detection

keynote.title: The local dark matter phase-space density and impact on WIMP direct detection
keynote.core_problem: Astrophysical uncertainty in the local dark matter phase-space density, particularly its deviation from the standard Maxwell-Boltzmann approximation, and its impact on WIMP direct detection rates and exclusion limits.
contributions: New determination of the local dark matter phase-space density using Eddington's inversion formula under isotropic and spherical symmetry assumptions
Bayesian inference framework with MCMC sampling of Milky Way mass models against diverse dynamical constraints
Quantitative comparison of phase-space densities across Einasto, NFW, and Burkert density profiles
Assessment of impact on WIMP scattering rates and direct detection exclusion limits, especially for light and inelastically scattering dark matter
methods: Eddington's inversion formula (under isotropic velocity distribution and spherical symmetry)
Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling of Milky Way mass models
Comparison of three dark matter density profiles: Einasto, NFW, Burkert
experiments_or_evidence: Sampling against a broad and variegated sample of dynamical constraints
Comparison of derived velocity dispersion and escape velocity with stellar kinematics estimates
Evaluation of scattering rates and exclusion limits for sample WIMP candidates (light mass, inelastic scattering)
assumptions: Three specific parametric dark matter density profiles (Einasto, NFW, Burkert)
datasets_or_materials: Broad and variegated sample of dynamical constraints (no specific datasets named)

important_claims.claim: Velocity dispersion tends to be larger for the Burkert profile compared to Einasto and NFW.
important_claims.evidence: Found via Bayesian MCMC sampling under Eddington inversion.

important_claims.claim: Escape velocity depends very weakly on the choice of density profile, with mean value in very good agreement with stellar kinematics estimates.
important_claims.evidence: Derived from the same Bayesian analysis and compared to stellar kinematics.

important_claims.claim: Phase-space densities differ significantly — especially in high-velocity tails — from the standard Maxwell-Boltzmann approximation with circular-velocity-based dispersion and sharp escape cutoff.
important_claims.evidence: Direct comparison performed in the paper.

important_claims.claim: Adopting self-consistent phase-space densities yields larger scattering rates and stronger exclusion limits than the Maxwell-Boltzmann approximation, especially for light and inelastically scattering WIMPs.
important_claims.evidence: Discussed via sample cases evaluating impact on detection rates and limits.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides tools for applying the derived phase-space density to other dark matter candidates or experimental setups; enables more astrophysically grounded reinterpretation of direct detection results.

keynotes.title: WIMP and FIMP dark matter in the inert doublet plus singlet model

keynote.title: WIMP and FIMP dark matter in the inert doublet plus singlet model
keynote.core_problem: Explaining multi-component dark matter where one component is feebly interacting (FIMP) and the other weakly interacting (WIMP), while satisfying theoretical, collider, cosmological, and astrophysical constraints.
contributions: Proposes a multi-component DM model with inert scalar doublet and complex scalar singlet under Z4 symmetry
Identifies viable parameter space consistent with relic density, direct/indirect detection, collider bounds, and theoretical constraints
Analyzes scenarios where FIMP dominates DM abundance, suppressing WIMP astrophysical signatures
Explores decay of inert doublet into singlet pairs, leaving only FIMP as DM, constrained by Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN)
methods: Parameter space scanning under theoretical constraints (e.g., perturbativity, stability)
Relic density calculation for both WIMP and FIMP components
Application of direct detection (e.g., XENON), indirect detection (e.g., Fermi-LAT), and collider (e.g., LHC) limits
Analysis of BBN constraints on late decays in the FIMP-only scenario
experiments_or_evidence: Consistency checks against Planck-measured relic density
Bounds from LHC searches for invisible decays or exotic scalars
Direct detection limits from experiments like XENON
Indirect detection limits from gamma-ray and cosmic-ray observations (e.g., Fermi-LAT)
assumptions: Discrete Z4 symmetry stabilizing both DM components
Thermal vs. non-thermal (freeze-in) production mechanisms for WIMP and FIMP respectively
Perturbative regime for couplings and scalar potential parameters
limitations: Astrophysical signatures of WIMP are suppressed when FIMP dominates, limiting detectability via direct/indirect detection
BBN constraints restrict viability of doublet-to-singlet decay scenarios
Collider probes become primary discovery channel in suppressed-astrophysics regimes

important_claims.claim: When FIMP forms the dominant DM component, astrophysical signatures of the WIMP are much suppressed.
important_claims.evidence: Astrophysical signatures of the WIMP are much suppressed when FIMP dominates, limiting detectability via indirect and direct detection

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a template for constructing and constraining hybrid WIMP-FIMP models with multiple scalar sectors and discrete symmetries; highlights interplay between collider and cosmological probes when astrophysical signals are suppressed.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: review of observational determinations and simulated Milky Way-like galaxies
systematic assessment of astrophysical uncertainties affecting WIMP direct detection interpretations
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:
The differential event rate in weakly interacting massive particle (WIMP) direct detection experiments depends on the local dark matter density and velocity distribution.
Finally, we conclude with an overview of various methods for handling these astrophysical uncertainties.
Full text excerpt: systematic assessment of astrophysical uncertainties affecting WIMP direct detection interpretations No explicit limitation extracted.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: sensitivity forecasting using instrument simulation and astrophysical modeling
improved constraints on M* and M_med via combined CTAO, direct detection, and collider analysis with FIRE-2–informed astrophysical inputs
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

This paper explores the sensitivity of the Cherenkov Telescope Array Observatory to dark matter annihilation in the Galactic Center, within the frameworks of Effective Field Theory and Simplified Models.

A key aspect of our work is the inclusion of updated dark matter density profiles, J-factors, and velocity dispersion distributions derived from the FIRE-2 cosmological hydrodynamical simulations, which significantly impact the expected indirect detection signals.

Furthermore, we update the constraints from direct detection experiments (Xenon1T and LZ) taking into account the astrophysical uncertainties informed by the FIRE-2 simulations, and also investigate limits coming from collider searches (ATLAS and CMS).

Our analysis reveals improved constraints on the effective suppression scale (M *) in the Effective Field Theory framework and on the mediator mass (M med) in Simplified Models compared to previous studies, highlighting the complementarity of the Cherenkov Telescope Array Observatory with direct and...[truncated]

We discuss the implications of these results for various dark matter interaction types, including scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, and axial-vector mediators, and emphasize the importance of considering realistic astrophysical inputs in interpreting dark matter search results across different experimen...[truncated]
Full text excerpt: improved constraints on M* and M_med via combined CTAO, direct detection, and collider analysis with FIRE-2–informed astrophysical inputs No explicit limitation extracted.

New self-consistent determination of local dark matter phase-space density differing significantly from standard Maxwell-Boltzmann approximation, leading to larger scattering rates and stronger exclusion limits
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

This result is obtained implementing, in the limit of isotropic velocity distribution and spherical symmetry, Eddington's inversion formula, which links univocally the dark matter distribution function to the density profile, and applying, within a Bayesian framework, a Markov Chain Monte Carlo algo...[truncated]

The derived dark matter phase-space densities differ significantly — most dramatically in the high velocity tails — from the model usually taken as a reference in dark matter detection studies, a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution with velocity dispersion fixed in terms of the local circular velocity an...[truncated]
Tools for applying our result on the local dark matter phase-space density to other dark matter candidates or experimental setups are provided.
